heroOverview: Megalencephaly (ICD-10: Q04.5) is a congenital condition in which the brain is abnormally large and heavy, with a weight or volume more than 2.5 standard deviations above the mean for age and sex, due to an increase in the actual cellular content of the brain rather than the accumulation of fluid. It occurs in approximately 2-4% of children evaluated for macrocephaly (large head circumference) and represents a distinct biological process from hydrocephalus, in which the head enlarges because of excessive cerebrospinal fluid. Megalencephaly is subdivided into metabolic megalencephaly, caused by storage disorders and metabolic enzyme defects, and anatomic megalencephaly, arising from overgrowth of brain cells driven by somatic or germline mutations in mTOR pathway and related genes such as PTEN, PIK3CA, and AKT3. Clinical consequences include intellectual disability, epilepsy, autism spectrum features, and macrocephaly, with severity varying substantially by cause and the extent of accompanying cortical dysplasia. Early genetic diagnosis enables targeted surveillance and, in selected mTOR-driven cases, the possibility of pathway-specific treatment.

keyStats.prevalence: Megalencephaly accounts for approximately 2-4% of referred macrocephaly cases; PTEN-related megalencephaly prevalence estimated at 1 in 200,000
keyStats.demographics: Affects males and females; benign familial megalencephaly (constitutional macrocephaly) is more common in males; PTEN mutations slightly male-predominant in published series
keyStats.avgAge: Often identified in early childhood when head circumference measurements cross percentile lines or exceed the 98th percentile
keyStats.globalCases: Exact global prevalence of pathological megalencephaly is uncertain; megalencephaly-capillary malformation-polymicrogyria (MCAP) syndrome reported in several hundred cases worldwide

definition: Megalencephaly (ICD-10: Q04.5) is defined as a brain with weight or volume exceeding 2.5 standard deviations above the age- and sex-matched mean due to an increase in the number or size of neurons and glial cells, rather than accumulation of cerebrospinal fluid or infiltration by abnormal tissue. The condition is classified into two broad categories. Metabolic (or dysmetabolic) megalencephaly arises when enzyme deficiencies cause progressive accumulation of abnormal substrates within brain cells; examples include Alexander disease, glutaric aciduria type 1, Canavan disease, L-2-hydroxyglutaric aciduria, and organic acidaemias. Anatomic megalencephaly reflects true brain overgrowth and is further subdivided into syndromic and non-syndromic forms. Syndromic anatomic megalencephaly occurs in recognisable overgrowth syndromes including PTEN hamartoma tumour syndrome (Cowden, Bannayan-Riley-Ruvalcaba), megalencephaly-capillary malformation-polymicrogyria (MCAP) syndrome, and megalencephaly-polymicrogyria-polydactyly-hydrocephalus (MPPH) syndrome. Non-syndromic anatomic megalencephaly is driven by somatic gain-of-function mutations in components of the PI3K-AKT-mTOR signalling pathway — including PIK3CA, PIK3R2, AKT3, and MTOR — which regulate neuronal and glial cell proliferation during brain development. Constitutional or benign familial megalencephaly, the most common form encountered in paediatric practice, reflects genetic predisposition to a larger but structurally normal brain and does not cause neurological dysfunction; it is transmitted as an autosomal dominant trait and detected by measuring a parent's head circumference. The condition is managed by Genetics, paediatric Neurology, and metabolic medicine depending on the subtype.

typesClassification.description: The most common form; brain is large but structurally normal. Head circumference is above the 98th percentile but tracks consistently; one parent also has a large head. No neurological dysfunction is expected. No treatment is required beyond reassurance and monitoring.

Germline PTEN mutations cause progressive megalencephaly, macrocephaly, autism spectrum features, intellectual disability, lipomas, and a substantially elevated lifetime risk of breast, thyroid, endometrial, and colorectal cancer. Macrocephaly (> +4 SD) is often the presenting feature in children.

Caused by somatic PIK3CA mutations; features include megalencephaly, cutaneous capillary malformations, polymicrogyria, digital abnormalities, and body asymmetry. Intellectual disability and epilepsy are common.

Caused by somatic or germline mutations in PIK3R2, AKT3, or CCND2; presents with megalencephaly, cortical malformations, postaxial polydactyly, and hydrocephalus. Severe intellectual disability and epilepsy are characteristic.

Brain enlargement secondary to intracellular substrate accumulation in disorders such as Alexander disease (GFAP mutations), Canavan disease (ASPA mutations), glutaric aciduria type 1, and L-2-hydroxyglutaric aciduria. Progressive neurological deterioration is the rule.

primarySymptoms: Macrocephaly — head circumference consistently above the 98th percentile or crossing centile lines upward — is the cardinal clinical finding that prompts investigation for megalencephaly.

Intellectual disability ranging from mild to profound depending on the subtype; benign familial megalencephaly has normal cognition while MCAP, MPPH, and metabolic forms produce significant impairment.
Epilepsy, present in 50-80% of children with anatomic megalencephaly syndromes; seizure types vary from focal to generalised and may be refractory to standard anticonvulsants.

Autism spectrum features — social communication difficulties, repetitive behaviours, and sensory sensitivities — are especially prominent in PTEN-related megalencephaly, affecting approximately 15-20% of individuals with macrocephaly and PTEN mutations.
Hypotonia (low muscle tone) in infancy, causing delayed achievement of motor milestones such as rolling, sitting, and walking.
Developmental delay, particularly in language and motor domains, identifiable from the first year of life in syndromic forms.
Hemimegalencephaly pattern (asymmetric brain enlargement), where one hemisphere is disproportionately larger, producing contralateral hemiparesis and severe ipsilateral seizures.
Frontal bossing and prominent forehead, visible inspection feature of accelerated head growth in infancy.
Cutaneous capillary malformations (port-wine stains) over the midface or body, a distinctive marker of MCAP syndrome caused by PIK3CA mosaicism.
Polydactyly (extra digits, usually postaxial) in MPPH syndrome, providing a clinical clue to AKT3/PIK3R2 mutations.
earlyWarningSigns: Head circumference crossing upward through percentile lines between birth and 12 months, even when the fontanelle appears normal.
Failure to meet expected developmental milestones alongside an unusually large head.
Multiple skin lesions (lipomas, angiofibromas, or capillary malformations) appearing together with macrocephaly.
A parent noted to have an unusually large head with a history of learning difficulties, suggesting familial PTEN or anatomic megalencephaly.
emergencySigns: Rapidly enlarging head circumference with bulging fontanelle, sunset sign, or irritability indicating acutely raised intracranial pressure — requires same-day neurosurgical assessment.
Prolonged focal or generalised seizures lasting more than five minutes without stopping (status epilepticus).
Acute neurological regression — sudden loss of previously acquired motor or cognitive skills — in a child with known metabolic megalencephaly, suggesting metabolic decompensation.
Signs of intracranial hypertension: vomiting, papilloedema, altered consciousness, or a bulging fontanelle.

causes.description: Loss-of-function mutations in PTEN, a phosphatase that negatively regulates the PI3K-AKT-mTOR pathway, result in unopposed mTOR signalling, driving excessive neuronal and glial proliferation. PTEN mutations cause the largest head circumferences seen in any single-gene disorder and confer substantially elevated cancer risks throughout life.

Mosaic activating mutations in PIK3CA, the catalytic subunit of phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase, promote cell growth and proliferation in a subset of brain and skin cells. The mosaic distribution explains variable clinical expression from isolated macrocephaly to full MCAP syndrome with vascular malformations and cortical dysplasia.

causes.cause: AKT3 and PIK3R2 somatic or germline mutations
causes.description: Gain-of-function changes in AKT3 (downstream PI3K effector) and loss-of-function changes in PIK3R2 (regulatory PI3K subunit) each increase PI3K-AKT signalling, causing bilateral megalencephaly with cortical malformations; they are the main molecular causes of MPPH syndrome.

causes.cause: Metabolic enzyme deficiencies (Alexander disease, Canavan disease)
causes.description: In metabolic megalencephaly, enzyme defects (GFAP astrocyte dysfunction in Alexander disease; ASPA deficiency in Canavan disease) lead to myelin abnormalities, accumulation of N-acetylaspartate or other substrates, and reactive brain swelling. The brain enlarges progressively and neurological function deteriorates over time.

Deficiency of glutaryl-CoA dehydrogenase causes accumulation of glutaric and 3-hydroxyglutaric acids, producing progressive striatal injury and megalencephaly before clinical crisis; macrocephaly is present from birth in about 75% of affected children.

Constitutional large-brain inheritance, likely reflecting polygenic variation in brain volume rather than a pathological mechanism; the brain is structurally normal and no abnormal accumulation or pathway activation is identified. A parent or first-degree relative with a large head is the diagnostic clue.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of macrocephaly or megalencephaly
riskFactors.description: Autosomal dominant PTEN mutations and benign familial megalencephaly both cluster in families; a parent or sibling with head circumference above the 97th percentile significantly raises prior probability.

PTEN mutations produce the highest macrocephaly Z-scores of any single-gene cause (often exceeding +4 SD), conferring both megalencephaly and substantially elevated lifetime cancer risk; parental PTEN testing should follow an affected child's diagnosis.
Somatic gain-of-function mutations arising during early embryogenesis lead to mosaic brain overgrowth; these are not inherited but occur as new post-zygotic events and are not preventable.

riskFactors.factor: Metabolic disorder in the family (Canavan, Alexander, organic acidaemia)
riskFactors.description: Metabolic megalencephaly conditions such as Canavan disease (autosomal recessive, ASPA mutations) and glutaric aciduria type 1 (autosomal recessive, GCDH mutations) carry a 25% recurrence risk for further pregnancies when both parents are carriers.
Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome and Simpson-Golabi-Behmel syndrome cause macrosomia including megalencephaly, mediated by IGF2 pathway dysregulation; these are identifiable prenatally or at birth.

riskFactors.factor: Autism spectrum diagnosis in a child with macrocephaly
riskFactors.description: Macrocephaly is present in approximately 15-20% of children with autism spectrum disorder; the combination substantially increases the probability of a pathogenic PTEN mutation, and PTEN testing is recommended by current clinical guidelines in this setting.

affectedDemographics: Infants and young children with accelerating head circumference growth
Children with autism spectrum disorder and macrocephaly (head circumference > +2.5 SD)
Individuals from families with autosomal dominant PTEN mutations
Children with metabolic disorders (Canavan, glutaric aciduria type 1, Alexander disease)
Neonates with extra digits (polydactyly) and large heads suggestive of MPPH syndrome
Males slightly more frequently affected in some anatomic subtypes, but both sexes are involved
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins when serial head circumference measurements show values above the 98th percentile or crossing percentile lines upward. The first step is to measure both parents' head circumferences: if a parent is similarly macrocephalic, benign familial megalencephaly becomes the most likely diagnosis and neuroimaging can often be deferred if the child is neurologically normal. When parental measurements are normal or neurological concerns exist, brain MRI is the definitive investigation. MRI distinguishes megalencephaly (enlarged brain parenchyma) from hydrocephalus (enlarged ventricles with normal-thickness cortex) and identifies key structural features: cortical malformations (polymicrogyria, pachygyria, cortical heterotopia), white matter signal abnormalities (suggesting metabolic cause), and vascular anomalies. Metabolic screening — urine organic acids, plasma amino acids, acylcarnitines, urine N-acetylaspartate, lysosomal enzyme panel — is essential when metabolic megalencephaly is suspected, particularly in children with progressive course or white matter changes on MRI. Genetic testing follows the clinical phenotype: in children with macrocephaly and autism or multiple lipomas, PTEN sequencing and deletion/duplication analysis is the priority (yield approximately 15-25% in macrocephaly-autism cohorts). In children with cortical malformations and somatic features of MCAP or MPPH, next-generation sequencing panels for PIK3CA, PIK3R2, AKT3, and MTOR — including deep sequencing of blood and ideally affected tissue — identify pathogenic variants. EEG is performed in all children with suspected or confirmed epilepsy. Ophthalmology review (for Lhermitte-Duclos disease in PTEN carriers) and dermatology review (for cutaneous features of PTEN or MCAP syndromes) are part of the syndromic workup.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Tracks head growth trajectory; values consistently above the 98th percentile or crossing upward through percentile lines are the primary trigger for further investigation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A flexible tape measure is placed around the largest part of the head at clinic visits; results are plotted on age- and sex-specific growth charts. Parental head measurements are obtained at the same visit.

diagnosticTests.test: Brain MRI with and without gadolinium
diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes megalencephaly from hydrocephalus; characterises cortical malformations, white matter abnormalities, and vascular anomalies that indicate the underlying aetiology.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The child lies in an MRI scanner for 30-60 minutes; young children or those who cannot cooperate usually require sedation or brief general anaesthesia. No radiation is involved.

diagnosticTests.test: PTEN gene sequencing and deletion/duplication analysis
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies pathogenic PTEN variants in children with macrocephaly plus autism, multiple lipomas, or other features of PTEN hamartoma tumour syndrome; mandated by clinical guidelines when macrocephaly exceeds +2.5 SD with ASD.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample is sent for molecular genetic analysis; results take 2-6 weeks. Positive results require parental testing and referral to a clinical geneticist.

diagnosticTests.test: PI3K-AKT-mTOR pathway gene panel / whole-exome sequencing
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects PIK3CA, PIK3R2, AKT3, MTOR, and CCND2 mutations in children with syndromic anatomic megalencephaly (MCAP, MPPH); deep sequencing improves detection of low-level somatic mosaicism.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample (and sometimes saliva or skin biopsy for mosaic analysis) is sequenced at a specialist laboratory; results take 4-8 weeks.

diagnosticTests.test: Urine organic acids and plasma acylcarnitine profile
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for metabolic causes of megalencephaly including glutaric aciduria type 1, Canavan disease (urine N-acetylaspartate), organic acidaemias, and lysosomal storage disorders.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A urine sample and a small blood draw are collected, preferably when the child is clinically stable; results from a specialist metabolic laboratory take 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterises epileptiform activity and seizure type in children with megalencephaly and epilepsy, guiding anticonvulsant selection and monitoring treatment response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Scalp electrodes record brain electrical activity for 20-60 minutes; the child should be drowsy or asleep for the most informative recording in young children.

treatmentOverview: There is no pharmacological treatment that reduces brain size in megalencephaly. Management targets the neurological, metabolic, and oncological consequences of the specific underlying subtype. Epilepsy, present in the majority of children with syndromic anatomic megalencephaly, is treated initially with standard anticonvulsants — levetiracetam, vigabatrin, sodium valproate, and lamotrigine — chosen according to seizure type and the child's metabolic status. mTOR pathway inhibitors such as everolimus and sirolimus show activity in mTOR-driven epilepsies and are used off-label in refractory cases associated with PIK3CA, AKT3, or MTOR mutations, with approximately 40% of patients achieving at least 50% seizure reduction. For PTEN hamartoma tumour syndrome, structured cancer surveillance protocols — annual thyroid ultrasound from childhood, breast MRI from age 25-30 in women, and colonoscopy from age 35 — form the most critical long-term management strand and must begin at diagnosis regardless of current tumour status, because cumulative lifetime cancer risk exceeds 85%. Neurodevelopmental rehabilitation — physiotherapy, occupational therapy, and speech-language pathology — from the first year of life maximises functional development through cortical plasticity. In hemimegalencephaly, surgical disconnection or hemispherectomy is considered for intractable seizures, achieving seizure freedom in approximately 50-70% of selected patients. Metabolic megalencephaly requires disease-specific strategies: glutaric aciduria type 1 is managed with dietary lysine restriction and L-carnitine; Canavan disease management is supportive with gene therapy trials ongoing. Hydrocephalus complicating MPPH syndrome requires VP shunt placement. Genetic counselling and cascade family testing are essential after any pathogenic variant is confirmed.

medicalTreatments.name: mTOR inhibitors (everolimus, sirolimus)
medicalTreatments.description: Rapamycin-analogue inhibitors of mTORC1 are used off-label in selected patients with megalencephaly caused by PI3K-AKT-mTOR pathway mutations; they target the mechanism driving cell overgrowth and show activity in reducing seizure frequency and, in some studies, reducing lesion growth.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In mTOR-related focal cortical dysplasias and TSC, everolimus reduces seizure frequency by 50% or more in approximately 40% of treated patients; data specific to MCAP/MPPH are limited.

medicalTreatments.name: Anticonvulsant therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Standard anticonvulsants (levetiracetam, vigabatrin, sodium valproate, lamotrigine) are the initial treatment for epilepsy; choice is guided by seizure type, EEG pattern, and the child's metabolic status. Approximately 50% of megalencephaly-associated epilepsy is drug-resistant.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: First anticonvulsant achieves seizure freedom in roughly 40-50% of affected children; drug-resistant cases require multi-drug approaches or ketogenic diet.

medicalTreatments.name: PTEN cancer surveillance programme
medicalTreatments.description: For PTEN mutation carriers, structured surveillance includes annual thyroid ultrasound from childhood, annual dermatology review, breast MRI and mammography from age 25-30 in women, and colonoscopy from age 35. Early tumour detection reduces cancer-related mortality.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Surveillance detects PTEN-related cancers at earlier stages; evidence from cohort studies shows improved outcomes compared with symptomatic presentation, though randomised trial data are not available.

medicalTreatments.name: Dietary management for metabolic megalencephaly
medicalTreatments.description: Glutaric aciduria type 1 is managed with dietary lysine restriction and L-carnitine supplementation; the goal is to prevent striatal injury during febrile illness by providing emergency glucose (sick-day protocols). Canavan disease dietary management is supportive with high-fat, low-aspartate approaches.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In glutaric aciduria type 1, newborn screening and early dietary management reduces the proportion of children with acute striatal injury from approximately 90% to below 10%.

surgicalOptions.name: Hemispherectomy / functional hemispherectomy
surgicalOptions.description: For children with hemimegalencephaly (unilateral brain overgrowth) and medically refractory seizures, surgical disconnection or removal of the affected hemisphere can dramatically reduce seizure burden. Performed at specialist paediatric neurosurgery centres.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Seizure freedom achieved in approximately 50-70% of appropriately selected hemimegalencephaly patients; motor deficits on the contralateral side are expected but functional recovery in young children can be substantial.

surgicalOptions.name: Ventriculoperitoneal shunt
surgicalOptions.description: Placed for progressive hydrocephalus complicating MPPH syndrome and occasionally other megalencephaly subtypes; diverts CSF to the abdominal cavity to relieve intracranial pressure.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Effective pressure relief in 80-85% of cases; shunt revision required in roughly 20-30% over 5 years due to obstruction or infection.

alternativeTreatments.name: Ketogenic diet for refractory epilepsy
alternativeTreatments.description: The high-fat, very-low-carbohydrate ketogenic diet is an evidence-based non-pharmacological option for drug-resistant epilepsy in children with megalencephaly syndromes; requires specialist dietetic supervision and metabolic monitoring.

alternativeTreatments.name: Early childhood intervention and neurodevelopmental therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured physiotherapy, occupational therapy, and speech-language pathology from the first year of life leverage cortical plasticity to maximise developmental gains; while not treatments for the underlying overgrowth, they meaningfully affect functional outcomes.

whySeeSpecialist: Megalencephaly is caused by diverse mechanisms — genetic, metabolic, and structural — that require different management strategies. A clinical geneticist is needed to identify the molecular cause, provide cancer risk counselling (critical for PTEN carriers), and coordinate family testing. A paediatric neurologist manages epilepsy, which is frequently severe and drug-resistant. A metabolic physician is required for the organic acidaemia and storage disorder subtypes. Without specialist input, PTEN-related cancer risk may go unrecognised for years, epilepsy may be suboptimally treated, and metabolic crises may be mismanaged.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a paediatric genetics or neurodevelopmental clinic at a children's hospital with access to clinical genetics, paediatric neurology, and metabolic medicine under one roof. For suspected PTEN syndrome, a clinical cancer genetics programme is essential. For hemimegalencephaly with refractory seizures, referral to a specialist paediatric epilepsy surgery centre is appropriate. Ask whether the team uses deep sequencing to detect somatic mosaicism, which standard blood-based tests can miss.
preventionStrategies: Pursue genetic counselling before future pregnancies when a pathogenic mutation (PTEN, ASPA, GCDH) has been identified in the family.
Newborn screening programmes for glutaric aciduria type 1 — where available — enable pre-symptomatic dietary treatment that prevents most striatal injury.
For known PTEN carriers, enrol in a structured cancer surveillance programme from the time of diagnosis regardless of age.
Prenatal testing (chorionic villus sampling or amniocentesis) for known familial mutations is available for autosomal recessive metabolic causes (Canavan, glutaric aciduria type 1).
Somatic PI3K-AKT pathway mutations are post-zygotic events and are not currently preventable, but their detection opens opportunities for mTOR inhibitor therapy.
lifestyleModifications: Establish a consistent daily routine to reduce seizure triggers — regular sleep and meal times, and careful management of fever with antipyretics.
Follow sick-day protocols: in glutaric aciduria type 1, fever requires emergency glucose supplementation and prompt medical review to prevent metabolic crisis and striatal injury.
Protect the child's head from injury during activities, particularly if hemimegalencephaly produces motor imbalance.
Maintain PTEN cancer surveillance appointments rigorously — cancer detection at an early, treatable stage is entirely dependent on regular scheduled screening.
Use support groups and neurodevelopmental services to maximise educational and social inclusion for children with cognitive impairment.

recommended: Lysine-restricted diet and L-carnitine supplementation for glutaric aciduria type 1
Balanced high-calorie diet to support growth in children with elevated metabolic demands from epilepsy and high muscle tone
Ketogenic diet under specialist supervision for drug-resistant epilepsy
Adequate vitamin D and calcium supplementation for children on long-term anticonvulsants that affect bone metabolism
avoid: High-lysine foods (excess meat, eggs, dairy) in glutaric aciduria type 1 beyond dietary prescription
Unprescribed high-dose supplements that may interact with anticonvulsant metabolism
Prolonged fasting in metabolic megalencephaly conditions, which can trigger catabolic crises

exerciseGuidelines: Children with megalencephaly should be encouraged toward age-appropriate physical activity, adapted to their motor capabilities. Physiotherapy guides safe exercise for children with hypotonia or hemimegalencephaly. Seizure precautions — supervised swimming, helmet use where head trauma risk is elevated — are appropriate for children with active epilepsy. Contact sports requiring extreme head protection should be discussed with the neurology team in children with large head circumference.
dailyManagement: Administer all prescribed anticonvulsants and metabolic medications at consistent times; do not miss doses during illness without medical advice.
Measure the child's head circumference at each routine clinic visit and plot on a growth chart to track the rate of growth.
Follow sick-day emergency protocols if metabolic megalencephaly is the diagnosis — fever triggers above-maintenance glucose and early medical contact.
Attend annual PTEN cancer surveillance if a PTEN mutation has been confirmed.
Keep a seizure diary recording frequency, duration, and type to guide medication adjustments at neurology reviews.
prognosis: Prognosis in megalencephaly varies enormously by subtype. Benign familial megalencephaly carries an excellent prognosis with normal cognition and lifespan. PTEN-related megalencephaly is associated with increased lifetime cancer risk — cumulative lifetime risk for any PTEN-related cancer exceeds 85% by age 70 in published series — but with structured surveillance, most cancers are detected at an early, treatable stage. Anatomic syndromic megalencephaly (MCAP, MPPH) is associated with moderate to severe intellectual disability and drug-resistant epilepsy in a majority of patients, with many requiring lifelong care. Hemimegalencephaly that undergoes successful surgical disconnection has the potential for meaningful seizure reduction and improved neurodevelopmental trajectory. Metabolic megalencephaly prognosis is disease-specific: glutaric aciduria type 1 detected by newborn screening and treated early carries a much better prognosis than late-detected cases that have already sustained striatal injury. Alexander disease and Canavan disease have a more progressive course, with deteriorating neurological function over years.
recoveryTimeline: Megalencephaly is a lifelong condition. In benign familial megalencephaly, no treatment is needed and no clinical deterioration occurs. For children with PTEN syndrome, cancer surveillance continues indefinitely from diagnosis. For syndromic anatomic megalencephaly, developmental gains are achieved gradually through early intervention programmes over years, not weeks. Epilepsy control is an ongoing process requiring regular review. Following hemispherectomy for hemimegalencephaly, intensive post-operative rehabilitation begins within days and continues for months to years to optimise neurological recovery.
complications: Malignancy in PTEN hamartoma syndrome — cumulative lifetime cancer risk exceeds 85% without surveillance; breast, thyroid, endometrial, and colorectal cancers are most common.
Refractory epilepsy — present in 50-80% of anatomic megalencephaly syndromes; drug-resistant seizures contribute heavily to morbidity and developmental regression.
Progressive hydrocephalus — a complication in MPPH syndrome and occasionally other subtypes; requires VP shunt placement.
Striatal injury in glutaric aciduria type 1 — acute neurological crisis during febrile illness causes irreversible basal ganglia damage and dystonia if not prevented by emergency protocols.
Musculoskeletal complications — hip dysplasia, scoliosis, and limb contractures develop in non-ambulant children with spasticity.
Psychosocial impairment — autism spectrum features and intellectual disability affect educational attainment and social relationships throughout life.

supportResources.description: Patient advocacy and research organisation supporting individuals with PTEN hamartoma tumour syndrome, including megalencephaly resources.

supportResources.name: NORD — Megalencephaly Capillary Malformation Syndrome
supportResources.description: Patient information on MCAP syndrome from the National Organization for Rare Disorders.

supportResources.name: NINDS — Brain Malformations Information Page
supportResources.description: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke overview of congenital brain malformations including megalencephaly.

confusedWithConditions.name: Congenital hydrocephalus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hydrocephalus enlarges the head by accumulating excess CSF, producing thinned cortex and dilated ventricles on MRI; megalencephaly shows increased brain parenchymal volume with normal or reduced ventricular size.

confusedWithConditions.name: Macrocephaly
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Macrocephaly is a descriptive term for any head circumference above the 98th percentile and has many causes including megalencephaly, hydrocephalus, and storage disorders; megalencephaly is one specific cause distinguished by actual brain overgrowth on MRI.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tuberous sclerosis complex
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tuberous sclerosis causes brain tubers, subependymal nodules, and seizures through TSC1/TSC2 mutations affecting mTOR signalling, and may produce some cortical thickening, but does not cause diffuse brain overgrowth in the way that PIK3CA or PTEN mutations produce megalencephaly.

relatedConditions.name: Holoprosencephaly
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are congenital brain malformations arising from disrupted brain developmental signalling; megalencephaly and HPE can co-occur in complex chromosomal syndromes.

relatedConditions.name: Tuberous sclerosis
relatedConditions.relevance: TSC mutations also affect the mTOR pathway; TSC and megalencephaly syndromes share pathway biology and similar drug targets (mTOR inhibitors).

relatedConditions.name: Epilepsy
relatedConditions.relevance: Epilepsy is a major complication of anatomic megalencephaly, present in 50-80% of cases, and is a primary driver of disability.

relatedConditions.name: Congenital hydrocephalus
relatedConditions.relevance: Hydrocephalus co-occurs with megalencephaly in MPPH syndrome and requires surgical management; distinguishing the two is a key diagnostic step.

faqs.question: What is megalencephaly?
faqs.answer: Megalencephaly is a condition where the brain is abnormally large due to more brain cells or larger cells, not excess fluid. The head circumference is above the 98th percentile for age. Causes range from harmless family traits to genetic syndromes affecting brain growth regulation.

faqs.question: What is the difference between megalencephaly and hydrocephalus?
faqs.answer: Megalencephaly means the brain tissue itself is oversized; hydrocephalus means CSF fluid has accumulated, enlarging the head but leaving the brain cortex thinned. Brain MRI distinguishes them clearly: megalencephaly shows large brain volume; hydrocephalus shows enlarged ventricles with a thin cortex.

faqs.question: What causes megalencephaly?
faqs.answer: The most common benign cause is familial megalencephaly, a harmless hereditary trait of a large but normal brain. Pathological causes include PTEN gene mutations, somatic PIK3CA or AKT3 mutations, and metabolic disorders like glutaric aciduria type 1 or Canavan disease that cause brain swelling through substrate accumulation.

faqs.question: Does megalencephaly always cause intellectual disability?
faqs.answer: No. Benign familial megalencephaly causes no cognitive impairment at all. Syndromic forms like MCAP or MPPH syndrome cause moderate to severe intellectual disability. PTEN-related megalencephaly is associated with autism spectrum features and learning differences but cognitive outcomes vary widely.

faqs.question: What is PTEN syndrome and how does it relate to megalencephaly?
faqs.answer: PTEN hamartoma tumour syndrome is caused by germline mutations in the PTEN gene, which regulates cell growth. It produces the largest head circumferences seen in any single-gene disorder, alongside autism features, benign tumours, and a substantially elevated lifetime cancer risk for breast, thyroid, and endometrial cancers.

faqs.question: How is megalencephaly diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis starts with serial head circumference measurements plotted on growth charts. When measurements exceed the 98th percentile without a macrocephalic parent, brain MRI is performed to distinguish brain overgrowth from hydrocephalus. Genetic testing targets PTEN, PIK3CA, AKT3, and other pathway genes based on clinical features.

faqs.question: Can megalencephaly be treated?
faqs.answer: There is no treatment to reduce brain size. Management targets complications: anticonvulsants or mTOR inhibitors for epilepsy, cancer surveillance protocols for PTEN mutations, dietary restriction for metabolic causes, and VP shunting for hydrocephalus. Early neurodevelopmental therapy improves functional outcomes.

faqs.question: What is MCAP syndrome?
faqs.answer: MCAP (megalencephaly-capillary malformation-polymicrogyria) syndrome is caused by somatic PIK3CA mutations. It combines megalencephaly with skin capillary malformations (port-wine birthmarks), cortical malformations, extra digits, and body asymmetry. Intellectual disability and epilepsy are common features.

faqs.question: What cancer risks come with PTEN-related megalencephaly?
faqs.answer: PTEN mutation carriers face a lifetime cancer risk exceeding 85%, with elevated rates of breast, thyroid, endometrial, and colorectal cancers. Structured surveillance, beginning at diagnosis in childhood, enables early detection at treatable stages.

faqs.question: Is megalencephaly hereditary?
faqs.answer: Some forms are. Benign familial megalencephaly is autosomal dominant; PTEN mutations can be inherited or arise as new mutations. Metabolic forms like Canavan disease are autosomal recessive with 25% recurrence risk. Somatic PIK3CA and AKT3 mutations arise spontaneously and are not typically inherited.

faqs.question: What is hemimegalencephaly?
faqs.answer: Hemimegalencephaly is a closely related condition where only one cerebral hemisphere is enlarged. It causes severe seizures on one side of the brain and often requires hemispherectomy surgery. It is associated with the same PI3K-AKT-mTOR pathway mutations as bilateral megalencephaly.

faqs.question: How is megalencephaly different from macrocephaly?
faqs.answer: Macrocephaly simply means a large head circumference and can result from megalencephaly, hydrocephalus, thickened skull, or storage disorders. Megalencephaly is one specific cause where the brain parenchyma itself is oversized, confirmed by brain MRI showing increased brain volume without excess ventricular fluid.

faqs.question: What specialist should evaluate a child with a large head?
faqs.answer: A paediatrician typically initiates evaluation by measuring parental head circumferences and arranging brain MRI. If brain overgrowth is confirmed, referral to clinical genetics and paediatric neurology is appropriate. Metabolic medicine is involved when metabolic storage disorders are suspected.

faqs.question: Can mTOR inhibitor drugs help in megalencephaly?
faqs.answer: mTOR inhibitors like everolimus target the signalling pathway driving overgrowth in PIK3CA, AKT3, and MTOR-related megalencephaly. They are used off-label and show reductions in seizure frequency of at least 50% in approximately 40% of treated mTOR pathway epilepsy patients.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis for a child with megalencephaly?
faqs.answer: Prognosis depends entirely on the cause. Benign familial megalencephaly has a normal prognosis. PTEN syndrome requires lifelong cancer surveillance but many patients live full lives. MCAP and MPPH syndromes cause significant disability. Metabolic forms detected early (especially glutaric aciduria type 1) have much better outcomes than those diagnosed after crisis.

faqs.question: Does a big head always mean megalencephaly?
faqs.answer: No. Most children with head circumference above the 98th percentile have benign familial macrocephaly, particularly if a parent also has a large head and the child is developing normally. Pathological causes are suspected when the head is growing faster than expected, neurological symptoms are present, or parental head sizes are normal.

faqs.question: What is glutaric aciduria and how does it cause megalencephaly?
faqs.answer: Glutaric aciduria type 1 is an autosomal recessive metabolic disorder where deficiency of glutaryl-CoA dehydrogenase causes toxic acid accumulation in the brain. About 75% of affected children have megalencephaly from birth. Febrile illnesses trigger striatal injury, causing irreversible movement disorder unless emergency glucose protocols are followed.

faqs.question: Is surgery needed for megalencephaly?
faqs.answer: Surgery is not used to reduce brain size. VP shunting treats hydrocephalus that complicates some megalencephaly syndromes. Hemispherectomy is considered in hemimegalencephaly when seizures are medically refractory and achieves seizure freedom in approximately 50-70% of appropriately selected children.

faqs.question: What should parents look for as early warning signs of megalencephaly?
faqs.answer: Parents should note if the child's head circumference is growing faster than expected between visits, crossing upward through percentile lines. Developmental delay, early seizures, or skin birthmarks alongside a large head should prompt evaluation. Measuring parental head circumferences at the same appointment is an inexpensive first step.

metaDescription: Megalencephaly is abnormal brain overgrowth from genetic mutations like PTEN or PIK3CA. Learn types, symptoms, diagnosis, cancer risks, and treatment.
